name: PyShark (Python Wrapper for TShark/Wireshark)

auth.notes: Local library; requires TShark/Wireshark installed; live capture may require root

performance.notes: TShark subprocess overhead; not suitable for high-speed line-rate capture

agent_readiness.notes: Python wrapper around TShark/Wireshark for packet capture and analysis. import pyshark. cap = pyshark.FileCapture('capture.pcap'). for pkt in cap: if 'TCP' in pkt: print(pkt.tcp.srcport). Live capture: cap = pyshark.LiveCapture(interface='eth0'). cap.sniff(timeout=10). cap.sniff_continuously(): for pkt in cap.sniff_continuously(): .... Filter: pyshark.FileCapture('file.pcap', display_filter='http'). BPF filter: capture_filter='tcp port 80'. Fields: pkt.ip.src, pkt.tcp.dstport, pkt['HTTP'].request_method. Async: pyshark.LiveCapture().sniff_continuously(). Decode as: pyshark.FileCapture(decode_as={'tcp.port==8080': 'http'}).

gotchas: TShark binary required — separate install (Wireshark package)
Live capture requires root/sudo or raw socket permissions on Linux
Subprocess-based — slower and more fragile than dpkt/scapy
Large PCAP files consume lots of memory when fully loaded
best_when: Analyzing PCAP files with Wireshark dissector knowledge (protocol parsing done by TShark)
Network forensics with display filter syntax from Wireshark
Quick packet analysis leveraging existing Wireshark/TShark filters
Security research reading existing PCAP captures
avoid_when: High-performance packet capture (use dpkt or pcapy with libpcap directly)
Production network monitoring (use tools with native packet capture)
summary: PyShark wraps TShark for Python PCAP analysis. FileCapture('file.pcap', display_filter='http'). LiveCapture(interface='eth0'). pkt.ip.src, pkt.tcp.dstport, pkt['HTTP'].request_method. Async sniff_continuously(). decode_as for protocol hints. MIT. Best for PCAP analysis using Wireshark dissectors. TShark binary required. Live capture needs root. Subprocess overhead. Use Scapy for crafting, dpkt for performance.
